From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

17th to 18th-century British political ideology

For other uses, see [Jacobite](/wiki/Jacobite_(disambiguation) "Jacobite (disambiguation)").

Not to be confused with the [Syriac Orthodox Church](/wiki/Syriac_Orthodox_Church "Syriac Orthodox Church") or the [Jacobite Syrian Christian Church](/wiki/Jacobite_Syrian_Christian_Church "Jacobite Syrian Christian Church").

Not to be confused with [Jacobin (politics)](/wiki/Jacobin_(politics) "Jacobin (politics)").

**Jacobitism**[[c]](#cite_note-3) was a political ideology advocating the restoration of the senior line of the [House of Stuart](/wiki/House_of_Stuart "House of Stuart") to the [British throne](/wiki/Monarchy_of_the_United_Kingdom "Monarchy of the United Kingdom"). When [James II of England](/wiki/James_II_of_England "James II of England") chose exile after the November 1688 [Glorious Revolution](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution "Glorious Revolution"), the [Parliament of England](/wiki/Parliament_of_England "Parliament of England") ruled he had "abandoned" the English throne, which was given to his [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") daughter [Mary II of England](/wiki/Mary_II_of_England "Mary II of England"), and his nephew, her husband [William III](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England").[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris2007271–272-4) In April 1689, on the same basis, the [Scottish Convention](/wiki/Convention_of_Estates_(1689) "Convention of Estates (1689)") awarded Mary and William the throne of Scotland.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarnes1973310–312-5)

The Revolution created the principle of a contract between monarch and people, which if violated meant the monarch could be removed. A key tenet of Jacobitism was that kings were appointed by God, making the post-1688 regime illegitimate; however, it also functioned as an outlet for popular discontent, and thus was a complex mix of ideas, many opposed by the Stuarts themselves. Conflict between [Prince Charles](/wiki/Charles_Edward_Stuart "Charles Edward Stuart") and Scottish Jacobites over the [Acts of Union 1707](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707") and [divine right](/wiki/Divine_right_of_kings "Divine right of kings") seriously undermined the [1745 rising](/wiki/Jacobite_rising_of_1745 "Jacobite rising of 1745").

Jacobitism was strongest in Ireland, the Western [Scottish Highlands](/wiki/Scottish_Highlands "Scottish Highlands"), [Perthshire](/wiki/Perthshire "Perthshire"), and [Aberdeenshire](/wiki/Aberdeenshire "Aberdeenshire").[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGooch199513-6) Pockets of support were also present in [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales"), [Northern England](/wiki/Northern_England "Northern England"), the [West Midlands](/wiki/West_Midlands_(region) "West Midlands (region)") and [South West England](/wiki/South_West_England "South West England"), all areas strongly [Royalist](/wiki/Cavalier "Cavalier") during the [Wars of the Three Kingdoms](/wiki/Wars_of_the_Three_Kingdoms "Wars of the Three Kingdoms"). In addition, the Stuarts received intermittent backing from countries like [France](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France"), usually dependent on their own strategic objectives.

In addition to the 1689–1691 [Williamite War in Ireland](/wiki/Williamite_War_in_Ireland "Williamite War in Ireland") and [Jacobite rising of 1689](/wiki/Jacobite_rising_of_1689 "Jacobite rising of 1689") in [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"), there were serious revolts in [1715](/wiki/Jacobite_rising_of_1715 "Jacobite rising of 1715"), [1719](/wiki/Jacobite_rising_of_1719 "Jacobite rising of 1719") and 1745, French invasion attempts in [1708](/wiki/Planned_French_invasion_of_Britain_(1708) "Planned French invasion of Britain (1708)") and [1744](/wiki/Planned_French_invasion_of_Britain_(1744) "Planned French invasion of Britain (1744)"), and numerous unsuccessful plots. While the 1745 Rising briefly seemed to threaten the [Hanoverian](/wiki/House_of_Hanover "House of Hanover") monarchy, its defeat in 1746 ended Jacobitism as a serious political movement.

## Political background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jacobitism&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Political background")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/2/26/True_Law_of_Free_Monarchies.jpg)](/wiki/File:True_Law_of_Free_Monarchies.jpg)

'The True Law of Free Monarchies;' [James VI and I](/wiki/James_VI_and_I "James VI and I")'s political tract formed the basis of Stuart ideology

Jacobite ideology originated with [James VI and I](/wiki/James_VI_and_I "James VI and I"), who in 1603 became the first monarch to rule all three kingdoms of [England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England"), [Scotland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland") and [Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland"). Its basis was [divine right](/wiki/Divine_right_of_kings "Divine right of kings"), which claimed his authority came from God, and the crown's descent by indefeasible hereditary right: James and his supporters emphasised his right to the throne by blood to forestall controversy over his appointment by [Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I "Elizabeth I") as her successor.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoldsworthy200178–79-7) His concept of personal rule eliminated the need for Parliaments, and required political and religious union, concepts widely unpopular in all three kingdoms, but especially England.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStephen201049-8)

The principle of divine right also clashed with Catholic allegiance to the [Pope](/wiki/Pope "Pope"), and with Protestant [nonconformists](/wiki/Nonconformist_(Protestantism) "Nonconformist (Protestantism)"), since both argued there was an authority above the king.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERyan1975122–124-9) The 17th-century belief that 'true religion' and 'good government' were one and the same meant disputes in one area fed into the other, while [Millenarianism](/wiki/Millenarianism "Millenarianism") and belief in the imminence of the [Second Coming](/wiki/Second_Coming "Second Coming") meant many [Protestants](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") viewed such issues as urgent and real.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJacob1976335–341-10)

As the first step towards union, James began standardising religious practices between the churches of [England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"), [Scotland](/wiki/Church_of_Scotland "Church of Scotland") and [Ireland](/wiki/Church_of_Ireland "Church of Ireland"). After his death in 1625, this was continued by his son [Charles I](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England"), who lacked his political sensitivity; by the late 1630s, instituting [Personal Rule](/wiki/Personal_Rule "Personal Rule") in 1629, enforcing [Laudian](/wiki/Laudian "Laudian") reforms on the Church of England, and ruling without [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_England "Parliament of England") led to a political crisis.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenyonOhlmeyer199812-11) Similar measures in Scotland caused the 1639–1640 [Bishops' Wars](/wiki/Bishops%27_Wars "Bishops' Wars"), and installation of a [Covenanter](/wiki/Covenanter "Covenanter") government.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenyonOhlmeyer199816-12)

Organised by a small group of Catholic nobility, the October 1641 [Irish Rebellion](/wiki/Irish_Rebellion_of_1641 "Irish Rebellion of 1641") was the cumulative effect of land confiscation, loss of political control, anti-Catholic measures and economic decline. The Rebellion was intended as a bloodless coup, but its leaders quickly lost control, leading to atrocities on both sides.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELenihan2001a20–23-13) In May, a Covenanter army landed in [Ulster](/wiki/Ulster "Ulster") to support Scots settlers. Although Charles and Parliament both supported raising an army to suppress the Rebellion, neither trusted the other with its control; these tensions ultimately led to the outbreak of the [First English Civil War](/wiki/First_English_Civil_War "First English Civil War") in August 1642.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenyonOhlmeyer199831-14)

In 1642, the [Catholic Confederacy](/wiki/Confederate_Ireland "Confederate Ireland") representing the Irish insurgents proclaimed allegiance to Charles, but the Stuarts were an unreliable ally, since concessions in Ireland cost them Protestant support in all three kingdoms. In addition, the [Adventurers' Act](/wiki/Adventurers%27_Act "Adventurers' Act"), approved by Charles in March 1642, funded suppression of the revolt by confiscating land from Irish Catholics, much of it owned by members of the Confederacy.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManganiello200410-15) The result was a three-way contest between the Confederacy, Royalist forces under the Protestant [Duke of Ormond](/wiki/James_Butler,_1st_Duke_of_Ormond "James Butler, 1st Duke of Ormond"), and a Covenanter-led army in Ulster. The latter were increasingly at odds with the English government; after Charles' execution in January 1649, Ormond combined these factions to resist the 1649-to-1652 [Cromwellian conquest of Ireland](/wiki/Cromwellian_conquest_of_Ireland "Cromwellian conquest of Ireland").[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELenihan2001b11–14-16)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Anthony_van_Dyck_-_Charles_I_%281600-49%29_with_M._de_St_Antoine_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/250px-Anthony_van_Dyck_-_Charles_I_%281600-49%29_with_M._de_St_Antoine_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:Anthony_van_Dyck_-_Charles_I_(1600-49)_with_M._de_St_Antoine_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

[Charles I](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England"), whose policies caused instability throughout his three kingdoms

In 1650, [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England") repudiated his alliance with the Confederacy in return for [Scottish military support](/wiki/Anglo-Scottish_war_(1650%E2%80%931652) "Anglo-Scottish war (1650–1652)"), and Ormond went into exile. Defeat resulted in the [mass confiscation](/wiki/Plantations_of_Ireland#Cromwellian_land_confiscation_(1652) "Plantations of Ireland") of Catholic and Royalist land, and its re-distribution among Parliamentarian soldiers and Protestant settlers.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELenihan2014140–142-17) The three kingdoms were combined into the [Commonwealth of England](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_England "Commonwealth of England"), only regaining their separate status following the 1660 [Stuart Restoration](/wiki/Stuart_Restoration "Stuart Restoration").[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWorden201063–68-18)

Charles II's reign was dominated by the expansionist policies of [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV") of France, seen as a threat to Protestant Europe. When his brother and heir [James](/wiki/James_II_of_England "James II of England") announced his conversion to Catholicism in 1677, an attempt was made to [bar him from the English throne](/wiki/Exclusion_Crisis "Exclusion Crisis").[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris1993b581–590-19) Nevertheless, he became king in February 1685 with widespread support from the Protestant majorities in England and Scotland. Accepting a Catholic monarch was seen as preferable to excluding the 'natural heir', and Protestant dissident rebellions in [England](/wiki/Monmouth_Rebellion "Monmouth Rebellion") and [Scotland](/wiki/Argyll%27s_Rising "Argyll's Rising") were quickly suppressed. It was also viewed as temporary, since James was 52, his second marriage was childless after 11 years, and his Protestant daughter [Mary](/wiki/Mary_II_of_England "Mary II of England") was heir.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller1978124–125-20)

His religion made James popular among Irish Catholics, whose position had not improved under his brother. Catholic land ownership had fallen from 90% in 1600 to 22% in 1685, partially due to Catholic landlords converting to the Protestant [Church of Ireland](/wiki/Church_of_Ireland "Church of Ireland"). After 1673, a series of proclamations deprived Catholic gentry of the [right to bear arms](/wiki/Right_to_keep_and_bear_arms "Right to keep and bear arms") or hold public office.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGrath199627–28-21) The Catholic [Richard Talbot, 1st Earl of Tyrconnell](/wiki/Richard_Talbot,_1st_Earl_of_Tyrconnell "Richard Talbot, 1st Earl of Tyrconnell"), was appointed [Lord Deputy of Ireland](/wiki/Lord_Deputy_of_Ireland "Lord Deputy of Ireland") in 1687, and began building a Catholic establishment that could survive James. Fearing a short reign, Tyrconnell moved at a speed that destabilised all three kingdoms.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris1993123–127-22)

When the English and Scottish Parliaments refused to remove civil restrictions on Catholics and Non-Conformists, James dismissed them and used the [Royal Prerogative](/wiki/Royal_Prerogative "Royal Prerogative") to force his measures through. These actions re-opened disputes over religion, rewarded the Protestant dissidents who rebelled in 1685, and undermined his own supporters. It also ignored the impact of the 1685 [Edict of Fontainebleau](/wiki/Edict_of_Fontainebleau "Edict of Fontainebleau"), which revoked tolerance for [French Protestants](/wiki/Huguenots "Huguenots") and created an estimated 400,000 refugees, 40,000 of whom settled in London.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpielvogel1980410-23) Two events turned discontent into rebellion, the first being the birth of [James's son](/wiki/James_Francis_Edward_Stuart "James Francis Edward Stuart") on 10 June 1688, which created the prospect of a Catholic royal dynasty. The second was James's prosecution of the [Seven Bishops](/wiki/Seven_Bishops "Seven Bishops"), which seemed to go beyond tolerance for Catholicism into actively attacking the Church of England. Their acquittal on 30 June caused widespread rejoicing, and destroyed James's political authority.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris2007235–236-24)

In 1685, many had feared civil war if James were bypassed. By 1688, even his chief minister, the [Earl of Sunderland](/wiki/Robert_Spencer,_2nd_Earl_of_Sunderland "Robert Spencer, 2nd Earl of Sunderland"), felt only his removal could prevent it. Sunderland secretly coordinated an [Invitation to William](/wiki/Invitation_to_William "Invitation to William"), assuring Mary and her husband and James's nephew, [William of Orange](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England") of English support for armed intervention. William landed in [Brixham](/wiki/Brixham "Brixham") on 5 November with 14,000 men; as he advanced, James's army deserted and he went into exile on 23 December.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris20073–5-25) In February 1689, the [English Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_England "Parliament of England") appointed William and Mary joint monarchs of England, while the [Scots](/wiki/Parliament_of_Scotland "Parliament of Scotland") followed suit in March.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECoward1980460-26)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/James_II_%281685%29.jpg/250px-James_II_%281685%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:James_II_(1685).jpg)

[James II](/wiki/James_II_of_England "James II of England"), 1685, dressed in military uniform

Most of Ireland was still controlled by Tyrconnell, where James landed on 12 March 1689 with 6,000 French troops, but the 1689-to-1691 [Williamite War in Ireland](/wiki/Williamite_War_in_Ireland "Williamite War in Ireland") highlighted two recurring trends. For James and his Stuart successors, the main prize was to regain England, while the primary French objective was to tie down British resources, rebellions in Scotland and Ireland being seen as the cheapest option.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcKay1983138–140-27) Elections in May 1689 produced the first [Irish Parliament](/wiki/Patriot_Parliament "Patriot Parliament") with a Catholic majority since 1613. It repealed the Cromwellian land seizures, confiscated land from Williamites, and proclaimed Ireland a 'distinct kingdom from England', measures subsequently annulled after defeat in 1691.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELenihan2014174–179-28)

A [Jacobite rising](/wiki/Jacobite_rising_of_1689 "Jacobite rising of 1689") in Scotland achieved some initial success but was ultimately suppressed. Several days after the Irish Jacobites were defeated at the [Battle of the Boyne](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Boyne "Battle of the Boyne") in July 1690, victory at [Beachy Head](/wiki/Battle_of_Beachy_Head_(1690) "Battle of Beachy Head (1690)") gave the French temporary control of the [English Channel](/wiki/English_Channel "English Channel"). James returned to France to urge an immediate invasion of England, but the Anglo-Dutch fleet soon regained maritime supremacy and the opportunity was lost.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELynn1999215-29)

The Irish Jacobites and their French allies were finally defeated at the [battle of Aughrim](/wiki/Battle_of_Aughrim "Battle of Aughrim") in 1691, and the [Treaty of Limerick](/wiki/Treaty_of_Limerick "Treaty of Limerick") ended the war in Ireland; future risings on behalf of the exiled Stuarts were confined to England and Scotland. The [Act of Settlement 1701](/wiki/Act_of_Settlement_1701 "Act of Settlement 1701") barred Catholics from the English throne, and when [Anne](/wiki/Anne,_Queen_of_Great_Britain "Anne, Queen of Great Britain") became the last Stuart monarch in 1702, her heir was her Protestant cousin [Sophia of Hanover](/wiki/Sophia_of_Hanover "Sophia of Hanover"), not her Catholic half-brother James. Ireland retained a separate Parliament until 1800, but the [1707 Union](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707") combined England and Scotland into the [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain"). Anne viewed this as the unified Protestant kingdom which her predecessors had failed to achieve.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESomerset2012532–535-30)

The exiled Stuarts continued to agitate for a return to power, based on the support they retained within the three kingdoms of England, Scotland and Ireland.[[28]](#cite_note-31)[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEÓ_Ciardha200021-32)[[30]](#cite_note-33) Doing so required external help, most consistently supplied by France, while Spain backed the [1719 Rising](/wiki/Jacobite_rising_of_1719 "Jacobite rising of 1719"). While talks were also held at different times with [Sweden](/wiki/Swedish_Empire "Swedish Empire"), [Prussia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Prussia "Kingdom of Prussia"), and [Russia](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire"), these never produced concrete results. Although the Stuarts were useful as a lever, their foreign backers generally had little interest in their restoration.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWills200157–58-34)

## Ideology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jacobitism&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Ideology")]

Historian [Frank McLynn](/wiki/Frank_McLynn "Frank McLynn") identifies seven primary drivers in Jacobitism, noting that while the movement contained "sincere men [...] who aimed solely to restore the Stuarts", it "provided a source of legitimacy for political dissent of all kinds".[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcLynn198299-35) Establishing the ideology of active participants is complicated by the fact that "by and large, those who wrote most did not act, and those who acted wrote little, if anything."[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELenman198036-36) As a result, historians have taken different views on its primary driving force. These include being an aristocratic rejection of an increasingly [unitary state](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state"), feudal opposition to capitalism, or Scots and Irish nationalism.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcLynn198298–99-37)

Jacobitism drew on elements of a [political theology](/wiki/Political_theology "Political theology") shared by mostly [non-juring](/wiki/Non-juring_schism "Non-juring schism") Tory elements within the Church of England, and members of the [Scottish Episcopal Church](/wiki/Scottish_Episcopal_Church "Scottish Episcopal Church").[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESzechi199492-38) These were the [divine right of kings](/wiki/Divine_right_of_kings "Divine right of kings"), their accountability to God, not man or Parliament; secondly, that monarchy was a divine institution; thirdly, [legitimism](/wiki/Dynastic_legitimists "Dynastic legitimists"), the crown's descent by indefeasible dynastic right, which could not be overturned or annulled; and lastly the scriptural injunction of passive obedience and non-resistance, even towards monarchs of which the individual subject might disapprove.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown200247-39)[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClark198589-40)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Alexander%2C_4th_Lord_Forbes_of_Pitsligo%2C_by_Alexis-Simon_Belle.jpg/250px-Alexander%2C_4th_Lord_Forbes_of_Pitsligo%2C_by_Alexis-Simon_Belle.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alexander,_4th_Lord_Forbes_of_Pitsligo,_by_Alexis-Simon_Belle.jpg)

[Alexander Forbes, 4th Lord Forbes of Pitsligo](/wiki/Alexander_Forbes,_4th_Lord_Forbes_of_Pitsligo "Alexander Forbes, 4th Lord Forbes of Pitsligo"); his support of the doctrine of indefeasible hereditary right placed him in a minority of Jacobites by 1745

Jacobite propagandists argued such divinely sanctioned authority was the main moral safeguard of society, while its absence led to [party](/wiki/Political_party "Political party") strife. They claimed the 1688 Revolution had enabled [political corruption](/wiki/Political_corruption "Political corruption") and allowed selfish opportunists, such as Whigs, religious dissenters, and foreigners, to take control of the government and oppress the common people.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMonod199392-41)

However, views on the 'correct' balance of rights and duties between monarch and subject varied, and Jacobites attempted to distinguish between 'arbitrary' and 'absolute' power. Non-juring [Church of Ireland](/wiki/Church_of_Ireland "Church of Ireland") [clergyman](/wiki/Anglican_clergyman "Anglican clergyman") [Charles Leslie](/wiki/Charles_Leslie_(nonjuror) "Charles Leslie (nonjuror)") was perhaps the most extreme divine right theorist, but even he argued the monarch was bound by "his oath to God, as well as his promise to his people" and "the laws of justice and honour".[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMonod199318-42) Jacobite pamphlets often suggested domestic issues were divine punishment on the British for rejecting their rightful king, although after 1710 this was blamed specifically on the [Whigs](/wiki/Whigs_(British_political_party) "Whigs (British political party)").[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMonod199328-43)

These views were not held by all Jacobites,[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEErskine-Hill198255-44) while many Whigs argued the post 1688 succession was also "divinely ordained".[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown200247-39)[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGibson201212-45) After the 1701 Act of Settlement, Jacobite propagandists deemphasised the purely legitimist elements in their writing and by 1745, active promotion of hereditary and indefeasible right was restricted largely to a few Scots Episcopalians such as Lords [Pitsligo](/wiki/Alexander_Forbes,_4th_Lord_Forbes_of_Pitsligo "Alexander Forbes, 4th Lord Forbes of Pitsligo") and [Balmerino](/wiki/Arthur_Elphinstone,_6th_Lord_Balmerino "Arthur Elphinstone, 6th Lord Balmerino").[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcLynn1982109-46)

Instead they began to focus on populist themes such as opposition to a standing army, [political corruption](/wiki/Political_corruption "Political corruption"), and social injustice.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEColley198528-47) By the 1750s, Charles himself promised triennial parliaments, disbanding the army and legal guarantees on [freedom of the press](/wiki/Freedom_of_the_press "Freedom of the press").[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESzechi199438-48) Such tactics broadened Jacobite appeal but also carried risks, since they could always be effectively neutered by a government prepared to offer similar concessions; indeed, the [Triennial Act](/wiki/Triennial_Act_1694 "Triennial Act 1694") and the lapse of the [Licensing of the Press Act](/wiki/Licensing_of_the_Press_Act_1662 "Licensing of the Press Act 1662") meant that regular Parliaments and (relative) press freedom were already in place after 1695.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEColley198529-49) The ongoing Stuart focus on England and regaining a united British throne led to tensions with their broader-based supporters in 1745, when the primary goal of most Scots Jacobites was ending the 1707 Union. This meant that following victory at [Prestonpans](/wiki/Battle_of_Prestonpans "Battle of Prestonpans") in September, they preferred to negotiate, rather than invade England as Charles wanted.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERiding2016199-50)

More generally, Jacobite theorists reflected a broader conservative current in [Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment") thought, appealing to those attracted to a monarchist solution to perceived modern decadence.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMonod199381-51) Populist songs and tracts presented the Stuarts as capable of correcting a wide range of ills and restoring social harmony, as well as contrasting Dutch and Hanoverian "foreigners" with a man who even in exile continued to consume English beef and beer.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESzechi199436-52) While particularly calculated to appeal to Tories, the wide range of themes adopted by Jacobite pamphleteers and agents periodically drew in disaffected Whigs and former radicals, particularly those interested in the issue of [constitutional reform](/wiki/Reform_Acts "Reform Acts"). Such "Whig-Jacobites" were highly valued by the court-in-exile, although many viewed James II as a potentially weak king from whom it would be easy to extract concessions in the event of a restoration, particularly with regard to the structure of Parliament.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESzechi199460-53)

## Jacobite supporters in the three kingdoms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jacobitism&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Jacobite supporters in the three kingdoms")]

### Ireland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jacobitism&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Ireland")]

The role of Jacobitism in Irish political history is debated; some argue that it was a broad-based popular movement and the main driver of Irish Catholic nationalism between 1688 and 1795.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEÓ_Ciardha200021,_30-54) Others see it as part of "a pan-British movement, rooted in confessional and dynastic loyalties", very different from 19th-century Irish nationalism.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConnolly201427–42-55) Historian Vincent Morely describes Irish Jacobitism as a distinctive ideology within the broader movement that "emphasised the [Milesian](/wiki/Milesians_(Irish) "Milesians (Irish)") ancestry of the Stuarts, their loyalty to Catholicism, and Ireland's status as a kingdom with a Crown of its own."[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorely2016333-56) In the first half of the 18th century, Jacobitism was "the primary allegiance of politically conscious Catholics".[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConnolly1992233–249-57)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/TyrconnellBrighter.jpg/250px-TyrconnellBrighter.jpg)](/wiki/File:TyrconnellBrighter.jpg)

[Tyrconnell](/wiki/Richard_Talbot,_1st_Earl_of_Tyrconnell "Richard Talbot, 1st Earl of Tyrconnell"), Deputy Governor of Ireland; his appointment of Catholics to military and political positions built widespread support for the Jacobite regime

Irish Catholic support for James was predicated on his religion and assumed willingness to implement (or at least inability to stop) their demands for Irish autonomy. In 1685, Irish [bard](/wiki/Bard "Bard") [Dáibhí Ó Bruadair](/wiki/D%C3%A1ibh%C3%AD_%C3%93_Bruadair "Dáibhí Ó Bruadair") celebrated his accession as ensuring the revived supremacy of both the [Catholic Church in Ireland](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Ireland "Catholic Church in Ireland") and the [Irish language](/wiki/Irish_language "Irish language"). Tyrconnell's expansion of the army by the creation of Catholic regiments was welcomed by Diarmuid Mac Carthaigh, as enabling the native Irish 'Tadhg' to be armed and to assert his dominance over "John" the English Protestant.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEÓ_Ciardha200077–79-58) Conversely, most Irish Protestants viewed his policies as designed to "utterly ruin the Protestant interest and the English interest in Ireland".[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELenihan2008175-59) This restricted Protestant Jacobitism to "doctrinaire clergymen, disgruntled Tory landowners and Catholic converts", who opposed Catholicism but still viewed James' removal as unlawful.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEÓ_Ciardha200089-60) A few Church of Ireland ministers refused to swear allegiance to the new regime and became [Non-Jurors](/wiki/Nonjuring_schism "Nonjuring schism"), the most famous being propagandist [Charles Leslie](/wiki/Charles_Leslie_(nonjuror) "Charles Leslie (nonjuror)").[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDoyle199729–30-61)

Since regaining England was his primary objective, James viewed Ireland as a strategic dead-end but [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV") of France argued it was the best place to start the counter-revolution, since the administration was controlled by Tyrconnell and his cause was popular among the majority Catholic population.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller1978220–221-62) James landed at [Kinsale](/wiki/Kinsale "Kinsale") in March 1689 and in May called the first [Parliament of Ireland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Ireland "Parliament of Ireland") since 1666, primarily seeking taxes to fund the war effort. Tyrconnell ensured a predominantly Catholic electorate and candidates by issuing new [borough](/wiki/Borough#Ireland "Borough") charters, admitting Catholics into city corporations, and removing "disloyal members".[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGillen201652-63) Since elections were not held in many northern areas, the [Irish House of Commons](/wiki/Irish_House_of_Commons "Irish House of Commons") was 70 members short, and 224 out of 230 [MPs](/wiki/Member_of_parliament "Member of parliament") were Catholic.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDoyle199730-64)

Known to 19th-century Irish historians as the "[Patriot Parliament](/wiki/Patriot_Parliament "Patriot Parliament")", it opened by proclaiming James as the rightful king and condemning the "treasonous subjects" who had ousted him. There were some divisions among Irish Jacobites on the issue of returning all Catholic lands confiscated [in 1652](/wiki/Act_for_the_Settlement_of_Ireland_1652 "Act for the Settlement of Ireland 1652") after the [Cromwellian conquest of Ireland](/wiki/Cromwellian_conquest_of_Ireland "Cromwellian conquest of Ireland"). The majority of the Irish House of Commons wanted the [1652 Cromwellian Act of Settlement](/wiki/Act_for_the_Settlement_of_Ireland_1652 "Act for the Settlement of Ireland 1652") repealed in its entirety, with ownership returned to that prevailing in 1641. This was opposed by a minority within the Catholic elite who had benefited from the [1662 Act of Settlement](/wiki/Act_of_Settlement_1662 "Act of Settlement 1662"), a group that included James himself, Tyrconnell and other members of the [Irish House of Lords](/wiki/Irish_House_of_Lords "Irish House of Lords"). Instead, they suggested those dispossessed in the 1650s should be restored to half their estates and paid compensation for the remainder.[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELenihan2008178-65) However, with the Commons overwhelmingly in favour of complete restoration, Tyrconnell persuaded the Lords to approve the bill.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELenihan2014136-66)

More serious was the fact James was unwilling to do anything that might jeopardise potential support from Protestants in England and Scotland.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELenihan2014136-66) These conflicted with the demands of the Irish Parliament, which in addition to land restoration included toleration for Catholicism and Irish autonomy.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris2007445-67) When it became clear Parliament would only vote war taxes if he met their minimum demands, James reluctantly gave his assent to Tyrconnell's land bill and passed a [bill of attainder](/wiki/Bill_of_attainder "Bill of attainder"), confiscating estates from 2,000 mostly Protestant "rebels".[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELenihan2014177-68) Although he also approved Parliament's resolution that Ireland was a "distinct kingdom" and laws passed in England did not apply there (this was already true), he refused to abolish [Poynings' Law](/wiki/Poynings%27_Law_(on_certification_of_acts) "Poynings' Law (on certification of acts)"), which denied the Irish Parliament the [right of initiative](/wiki/Right_of_initiative_(legislative) "Right of initiative (legislative)").[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMoodyMartinByrne2009490-69)

Despite his own Catholicism, James viewed the Protestant Church of Ireland as an important part of his support base; he insisted on retaining its legal pre-eminence, although agreeing landowners would only have to pay [tithes](/wiki/Tithes "Tithes") to clergy of their own religion.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELenihan2014177-68) However, the price of these concessions was that Irish Jacobitism became almost entirely confined to Catholics, who after 1690 split between Tyrconnell's 'peace party', who sought a negotiated solution, and a 'war party' led by [Patrick Sarsfield](/wiki/Patrick_Sarsfield "Patrick Sarsfield") who favoured fighting on to the end.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESimms1952309–312-70)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Uniform_and_colonel%E2%80%99s_flag_of_the_Hibernia_Regiment.jpg/250px-Uniform_and_colonel%E2%80%99s_flag_of_the_Hibernia_Regiment.jpg)](/wiki/File:Uniform_and_colonel%E2%80%99s_flag_of_the_Hibernia_Regiment.jpg)

The Spanish [Regiment of Hibernia](/wiki/Regiment_of_Hibernia "Regiment of Hibernia"), c. 1740; foreign military service remained common for Irish Catholics until banned after 1745

James left Ireland after defeat at [the Boyne](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Boyne "Battle of the Boyne") in 1690, telling his supporters to "shift for themselves".[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELenihan2008183-71) This led some to depict him as *Séamus an chaca*, "James the shit", who had deserted his loyal followers.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEÓ_Ciardha200084-72) However, Gaelic scholar [Breandán Ó Buachalla](/wiki/Breand%C3%A1n_%C3%93_Buachalla "Breandán Ó Buachalla") claims his reputation subsequently recovered as "the rightful king ... destined to return', while upper-class Irish Jacobites like [Charles O'Kelly](/wiki/Charles_O%27Kelly "Charles O'Kelly") and Nicholas Plunkett blamed "corrupt English and Scottish advisors" for his apparent desertion.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEÓ_Ciardha200085-73)

After 1691, measures passed by the 1689 Parliament were annulled, [penal laws](/wiki/Penal_Laws_(Ireland) "Penal Laws (Ireland)") criminalized the practice of Catholicism and barred Catholics from public life, while the Act of Attainder was used to justify further land confiscations. 12,000 Jacobite soldiers went into exile in the diaspora known as the [Flight of the Wild Geese](/wiki/Flight_of_the_Wild_Geese "Flight of the Wild Geese"), the majority of whom were later absorbed into the [French Army](/wiki/French_Royal_Army "French Royal Army")'s [Irish Brigade](/wiki/Irish_Brigade_(French) "Irish Brigade (French)"). About 1,000 men were recruited for the French and Spanish armies annually, many with a "tangible commitment to the Stuart cause".[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELenihan2014199-74) Elements of the French Irish Brigade participated in the Scottish Jacobite rising of 1745.

Irish-language poets, especially in [Munster](/wiki/Munster "Munster"), continued to champion the cause after James' death; in 1715, Eoin O Callanain described his son Prince [James Francis Edward Stuart](/wiki/James_Francis_Edward_Stuart "James Francis Edward Stuart") as *taoiseach na nGaoidheal* or "chieftain of the Gaels".[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorley2007194-75) As in England, throughout the 1720s, Prince James' birthday on 10 June was marked by celebrations in Dublin, and towns like [Kilkenny](/wiki/Kilkenny "Kilkenny") and [Galway](/wiki/Galway "Galway"). These were often accompanied by rioting, suggested as proof of popular pro-Jacobite sympathies.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELenihan2014244-76) Others argue riots were common in 18th-century urban areas and see them as a "series of ritualised clashes".[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGarnham200281–82-77)

Some historians claim Jacobite rhetoric and symbolism in the many works of [Aisling](/wiki/Aisling "Aisling") poetry composed in the [Irish language](/wiki/Irish_language "Irish language") and support for [rapparees](/wiki/Rapparees "Rapparees") like [Éamonn an Chnoic](/wiki/%C3%89amonn_an_Chnoic "Éamonn an Chnoic"), [John Hurley](/wiki/John_Hurley_(Jacobite) "John Hurley (Jacobite)"), and [Galloping Hogan](/wiki/Galloping_Hogan "Galloping Hogan"), is proof of popular backing for a Stuart restoration.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEÓ_Ciardha2000144-78) Others, however, argue it is hard to discern "how far rhetorical Jacobitism reflected support for the Stuarts, as opposed to discontent with the status quo".[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGillen201659-79) Nevertheless, fears of resurgent Jacobitism among the ruling Protestant minority meant anti-Catholic Penal Laws remained in place for most of the eighteenth century.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEÓ_Ciardha2000374-80)

In both 1715 and 1745, there were no Irish risings to accompany those in England and Scotland. One suggestion is that for various reasons, post 1691 Irish Jacobites looked to European allies, rather than a domestic revolt.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEÓ_Ciardha200084-72) From the 1720s on, many middle-class Catholics were willing to swear loyalty to the Hanoverian regime, but balked at the [Oath of Abjuration](/wiki/English_post-Reformation_oaths#The_Irish_Oath_of_1774_to_Catholic_Emancipation,_1829 "English post-Reformation oaths"), which required them to reject the authority of the Pope, and doctrines of [Transubstantiation](/wiki/Transubstantiation "Transubstantiation") and the [Real Presence](/wiki/Real_Presence "Real Presence").[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConnolly200364–65-81) After the effective demise of the Jacobite cause in the 1750s and '60s, organisations like the [Catholic Convention](/wiki/Catholic_Convention "Catholic Convention") were established to agitate for the redress of Catholic grievances before Parliament, detaching [Catholic emancipation](/wiki/Catholic_emancipation "Catholic emancipation") from Jacobitism, which was by then moribund as a cause.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGraham200251-82) When [Charles](/wiki/Charles_Edward_Stuart "Charles Edward Stuart") died in 1788, Irish nationalists looked for alternative liberators, among them the [French First Republic](/wiki/French_First_Republic "French First Republic"), [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon") Bonaparte and [Daniel O'Connell](/wiki/Daniel_O%27Connell "Daniel O'Connell").[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorley2007198–201-83)
