In England and Wales, Jacobitism was often associated with the [Tories](/wiki/Tories_(British_political_party) "Tories (British political party)"), many of whom supported James's right to the throne during the [Exclusion Crisis](/wiki/Exclusion_Crisis "Exclusion Crisis"). Tory positions on constitutional matters gave rise to the belief that neither "time nor statute law [...] could ameliorate the sin of usurpation",[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESzechi199464-84) while shared Tory and Jacobite themes of divine right and sacred kingship may have provided an alternative to [Whig](/wiki/Whigs_(British_political_party) "Whigs (British political party)") concepts of "liberty and property".[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown200262-85) A minority of academics, including [Eveline Cruickshanks](/wiki/Eveline_Cruickshanks "Eveline Cruickshanks"), have argued that until the late 1750s the Tories were a crypto-Jacobite party; others, that Jacobitism was a "limb of Toryism".[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcLynn198581-86) However, the supremacy of the Church of England was also central to Tory ideology, and James lost their support when his policies seemed to threaten that primacy. The [Act of Settlement 1701](/wiki/Act_of_Settlement_1701 "Act of Settlement 1701") excluding Catholics from the English throne was passed by a Tory administration; for most Tories, Stuart Catholicism was an insuperable barrier to active support, while the Tory doctrine of non-resistance also discouraged them from supporting the exiles against a reigning monarch, even one of dubious legitimacy.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcLynn198298-87)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Henry_St_John%2C_1st_Viscount_Bolingbroke_%281678%29.jpg/250px-Henry_St_John%2C_1st_Viscount_Bolingbroke_%281678%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Henry_St_John,_1st_Viscount_Bolingbroke_(1678).jpg)

Tory minister and Jacobite [Lord Bolingbroke](/wiki/Henry_St_John,_1st_Viscount_Bolingbroke "Henry St John, 1st Viscount Bolingbroke"); driven into exile in 1715 and pardoned in 1720

For most of the period from 1690 to 1714, Parliament was either controlled by the Tories, or evenly split with the Whigs; when [George I](/wiki/George_I_of_Great_Britain "George I of Great Britain") succeeded Anne, most hoped to reconcile with the new regime. The [Earl of Mar](/wiki/John_Erskine,_Earl_of_Mar_(1675%E2%80%931732) "John Erskine, Earl of Mar (1675–1732)"), who led the 1715 rising, observed "Jacobitisme, which they used to brand the Tories with, is now I presum out of doors".[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEColley198526-88) However, George blamed the Tory ministry for the [Peace of Utrecht](/wiki/Peace_of_Utrecht "Peace of Utrecht"), which he viewed as damaging to his home state of [Hanover](/wiki/Electorate_of_Hanover "Electorate of Hanover"). His isolation of former Tory ministers like [Lord Bolingbroke](/wiki/Henry_St_John,_1st_Viscount_Bolingbroke "Henry St John, 1st Viscount Bolingbroke") and the Earl of Mar drove them first into opposition, then exile. Their exclusion from power between 1714 and 1742 led many Tories to get into or remain in contact with the Jacobite court, which they saw as a tool to put pressure on the Whig government, and a last resort if campaigning by peaceable means failed.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcLynn1982107-89)

In 1715, there were co-ordinated celebrations on 29 May, [Restoration Day](/wiki/Oak_Apple_Day "Oak Apple Day"), and 10 June, James Stuart's birthday, especially in Tory-dominated towns like [Bristol](/wiki/Bristol "Bristol"), [Oxford](/wiki/Oxford "Oxford"), [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester") and [Norwich](/wiki/Norwich "Norwich"), although they remained quiet in the 1715 Rising. In the 1730s many 'Jacobite' demonstrations in Wales and elsewhere were driven by local sectarian tensions, especially hostility to [Methodism](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism"), and featured attacks on Nonconformist chapels.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERogers198270–88-90) Most English participants in 1715 came from traditionally Catholic areas in the [Northwest](/wiki/North_West_England "North West England"), such as [Lancashire](/wiki/Lancashire "Lancashire").[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOates201697–98-91) By 1720 there were fewer than 115,000 in England and Wales, and most remained loyal in 1745, including the [Duke of Norfolk](/wiki/Edward_Howard,_9th_Duke_of_Norfolk "Edward Howard, 9th Duke of Norfolk"), the acknowledged leader of the English Catholics, who was sentenced to death for his role in 1715, but later pardoned.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYates201437–38-92) Even so, sympathies were complex; Norfolk's agent Andrew Blood joined the [Manchester Regiment](/wiki/Manchester_Regiment_(Jacobite) "Manchester Regiment (Jacobite)"), and he later employed another ex-officer, John Sanderson, as his master of horse.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMonod1993134-93) Some English Catholics continued to provide the exiles with financial support well into the 1770s.[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESzechi199418–19-94)

In 1689, around 2% of clergy in the Church of England refused to take the oath of allegiance to William and Mary; one list identifies a total of 584 clergy, schoolmasters and university dons as [nonjurors](/wiki/Nonjuring_schism "Nonjuring schism").[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOverton1902467–496-95) This understates their numbers, insofar as many sympathisers remained within the Church of England, but the small population of nonjurors were over-represented in Jacobite risings and riots, and provided many "martyrs". By the late 1720s arguments over doctrine and the death of much of its leadership reduced the nonjuring church to a handful of scattered congregations, but several of those executed in 1745 came from Manchester, the last significant nonjuring centre in England.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESzechi199419-96)

Support also came from quarters less obviously in tune with conservative monarchism; [Quaker](/wiki/Quaker "Quaker") leader [William Penn](/wiki/William_Penn "William Penn") was a prominent [Nonconformist](/wiki/Nonconformist_(Protestantism) "Nonconformist (Protestantism)") supporter of James, although this was based on their personal relationship and did not survive his deposition. Another element in English Jacobitism was a handful of disaffected [Radicals](/wiki/Radicals_(UK) "Radicals (UK)"), whose [left-wing](/wiki/Left-wing_politics "Left-wing politics") (for the time) ideology was at face value incompatible with Jacobite conservatism, but who saw the possibility of extracting [Parliamentary reform](/wiki/Parliamentary_reform "Parliamentary reform") from a new and weak dynasty, and possibly replacing the (presumably) displaced Whigs as a [major political party](/wiki/Major_party "Major party"); John Matthews, a Jacobite printer executed in 1719; his pamphlet *Vox Populi vox Dei* emphasised the [Lockean](/wiki/Lockean "Lockean") theory of the [social contract](/wiki/Social_contract "Social contract"), a doctrine very few Tories of the period would have supported.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEColley198528-47)

### Scotland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jacobitism&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Scotland")]

Scottish Jacobitism had wider and more extensive roots than in England. 20,000 Scots fought for the Jacobites in 1715, compared to 11,000 who joined the government army, and were the majority of the 9,000 to 14,000 who served in 1745.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESzechi199477-97) One reason was the persistence of feudalism in parts of rural Scotland, where tenants could be compelled to provide their landlords with military service. Many of the Highland [clansmen](/wiki/Scottish_clans "Scottish clans") who were a feature of Jacobite armies were raised this way: in all three major risings, the bulk of the rank and file were supplied by a small number of north-western clans whose leaders joined the rebellion.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcCann196320-98)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Lord_George_Murray.jpg/250px-Lord_George_Murray.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lord_George_Murray.jpg)

Jacobite commander [George Murray](/wiki/Lord_George_Murray_(general) "Lord George Murray (general)"); a pro-Union, anti-Hanoverian Scot who fought in the 1715, 1719 and 1745 Risings but loathed Prince Charles, he encapsulated the many contradictions of Jacobite support

Despite this, many Jacobites were Protestant Lowlanders, rather than the Catholic, Gaelic-speaking Highlanders of legend.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPittock1998135-99) By 1745, fewer than 1% of Scots were Catholic, restricted to the far north-west and a few noble families.[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHamilton19634-100) The majority of the rank and file, as well as many Jacobite leaders, belonged to Protestant non-juring Episcopalian congregations.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESzechi199467-101) Throughout the 17th century, the close connection between Scottish politics and religion meant [regime changes](/wiki/Regime_change "Regime change") were accompanied by the losers being expelled from the [Church of Scotland](/wiki/Church_of_Scotland "Church of Scotland"). In [1690](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution_in_Scotland#Religious_settlement "Glorious Revolution in Scotland"), over 200 clergymen lost their parishes, mostly in [Aberdeenshire](/wiki/Aberdeenshire "Aberdeenshire") and [Banffshire](/wiki/Banffshire "Banffshire"), a strongly Episcopalian area since the 1620s. In 1745, around 25% of Jacobite recruits came from this part of the country.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPittock199899-102)

Episcopalianism was popular among social conservatives, as it emphasised indefeasible hereditary right, absolute obedience, and implied deposition of the senior Stuart line was a breach of natural order.[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacinnes2007235-103) The church continued to offer prayers for the Stuarts until 1788, while many refused to swear allegiance to the Hanoverians in 1714.[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStrong200215-104) However, even in 1690, a substantial minority accommodated to the new regime, a number that increased significantly after the establishment of the [Scottish Episcopal Church](/wiki/Scottish_Episcopal_Church "Scottish Episcopal Church") in 1712.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESzechi199419–20-105)

Episcopalian ministers, such as Professor James Garden of Aberdeen, presented the 1707 Union as one in a series of disasters to befall Scotland, provoked by "the sins [...] of rebellion, injustice, oppression, schism and perjury".[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaw199989-106) Opposition was boosted by measures imposed by the post-1707 [Parliament of Great Britain](/wiki/Parliament_of_Great_Britain "Parliament of Great Britain"), including the [Treason Act 1708](/wiki/Treason_Act_1708 "Treason Act 1708"), the 1711 ruling that barred Scots peers with English or British peerages from their seats in the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords"), and tax increases.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESzechi199472-107) Despite their own preferences, the Stuarts tried to appeal to this group; in 1745, Charles issued declarations dissolving the "pretended Union", despite concerns this would alienate his English supporters.[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPittock199826-108)

However, opposition to post-Union legislation was not restricted to Jacobites. Many Presbyterians opposed the establishment of the Episcopal Church in 1712 and other measures of indulgence, while the worst [tax riots](/wiki/Malt_tax_riots "Malt tax riots") took place in [Glasgow](/wiki/Glasgow "Glasgow"), a town noted for its antipathy to the Stuarts.[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERiding2016337-109) As in England, some objected less to the Union than the Hanoverian connection; [Lord George Murray](/wiki/Lord_George_Murray_(general) "Lord George Murray (general)"), a senior Jacobite commander in 1745, was a Unionist who repeatedly disagreed with Charles, but opposed "wars [...] on account of the Electors of Hanover".[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcLynn1982109–110-110)

## Community

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jacobitism&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Community")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Floramacdonald2.jpg/250px-Floramacdonald2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Floramacdonald2.jpg)

[Flora MacDonald](/wiki/Flora_MacDonald "Flora MacDonald") by [Allan Ramsay](/wiki/Allan_Ramsay_(artist) "Allan Ramsay (artist)") c. 1749–1750; note white roses, a Jacobite symbol

While Jacobite agents continued in their attempts to recruit the disaffected, the most committed Jacobites were often linked by relatively small family networks, particularly in Scotland; Jacobite activities in areas like [Perthshire](/wiki/Perthshire "Perthshire") and [Aberdeenshire](/wiki/Aberdeenshire "Aberdeenshire") centred on a limited number of influential families heavily involved in 1715 and 1745.[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESzechiSankey200195–96-111)

Some of the most powerful landowning families preserved their establishment loyalties, but maintained traditions of Stuart allegiance by permitting younger sons to become involved in active Jacobitism; in 1745, [Lewis Gordon](/wiki/Lewis_Gordon_(Jacobite) "Lewis Gordon (Jacobite)") was widely believed to be a proxy for his brother, the [Duke of Gordon](/wiki/Cosmo_George_Gordon,_3rd_Duke_of_Gordon "Cosmo George Gordon, 3rd Duke of Gordon").[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELenman1980255-112) Many Jacobite leaders were closely linked to each other and the exile community by marriage or blood. This has led some historians, notably [Bruce Lenman](/wiki/Bruce_Lenman "Bruce Lenman"), to characterise the Jacobite risings as French-backed [coup](/wiki/Coup "Coup") attempts by a small network drawn from the elite, though this view is not universally accepted.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELenman1980256-113)

Family traditions of Jacobite sympathy were reinforced through objects such as inscribed glassware or rings with hidden symbols, although many of those that survive are in fact 19th-century neo-Jacobite creations. Other family heirlooms contained reference to executed Jacobite martyrs, for which the movement preserved an unusual level of veneration.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESzechi199436–37-114) [Tartan](/wiki/Tartan "Tartan") cloth, widely adopted by the [Jacobite army](/wiki/Jacobite_Army_(1745) "Jacobite Army (1745)") in 1745, was used in portraiture as a symbol of Stuart sympathies, even before the Rising. Outside elite social circles, the Jacobite community circulated propaganda and symbolic objects through a network of clubs, print-sellers and pedlars, aimed at the provincial gentry and middling sort. In 1745, Prince Charles ordered commemorative medals and miniature pictures for clandestine distribution.[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMonod199381–82-115)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Sir_Watkin_Williams%2C_3rd_Bt_of_Llanforda.jpg/250px-Sir_Watkin_Williams%2C_3rd_Bt_of_Llanforda.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sir_Watkin_Williams,_3rd_Bt_of_Llanforda.jpg)

Welsh Tory [Sir Watkin Williams-Wynn](/wiki/Sir_Watkin_Williams-Wynn,_3rd_Baronet "Sir Watkin Williams-Wynn, 3rd Baronet"); his blue coat was a colour often worn by Jacobite sympathisers

Among the more visible elements of the Jacobite community were drinking clubs established in the early 18th century, such as the Scottish Bucks Club or the "Cycle of the White Rose", led by Welsh Tory [Sir Watkin Williams-Wynn](/wiki/Sir_Watkin_Williams-Wynn,_3rd_Baronet "Sir Watkin Williams-Wynn, 3rd Baronet").[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELord200440-116) Others included the "Sea Serjeants", largely composed of South Wales gentry or the "Independent Electors of Westminster" led by the [Glamorganshire](/wiki/Glamorganshire "Glamorganshire") lawyer [David Morgan](/wiki/David_Morgan_(Jacobite) "David Morgan (Jacobite)"), executed for his role in 1745.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERiding2016378-117) Other than Morgan, the vast majority of their members took no part in the 1745 Rising; Charles later said "I will do for the Welsh Jacobites what they did for me. I will drink their health".[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPittock1997107-118)

[Oak Apple Day](/wiki/Oak_Apple_Day "Oak Apple Day") on 29 May commemorated Charles II and was an occasion for displays of Stuart sympathy, as was "White Rose Day", [the Old Pretender's](/wiki/James_Francis_Edward_Stuart "James Francis Edward Stuart") birthday on 10 June.[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMonod1993210-119) Symbols were commonly employed by Jacobites, given that they could not be prosecuted for their use; the most common of these was the [White rose of York](/wiki/White_rose_of_York "White rose of York"), adopted after 1688 for reasons now unclear. Various origins have been suggested, including its use as an ancient Scottish royal device, its association with James II as [Duke of York](/wiki/Duke_of_York "Duke of York"), or Charles I being styled as the "White King".[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPittock199872–73-120) Jacobite military units often used plain white standards or [cockades](/wiki/Cockade "Cockade"), while green ribbons were another recognised Stuart symbol despite their association with the Whig [Green Ribbon Club](/wiki/Green_Ribbon_Club "Green Ribbon Club").[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERogers198225-121)

## Post-1745 decline

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jacobitism&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Post-1745 decline")]

Despite being greeted as a hero on his return to Paris, Charles' reception behind the scenes was more muted. [D'Éguilles](/wiki/Alexandre_Jean-Baptiste_de_Boyer,_Marquis_d%27%C3%89guilles "Alexandre Jean-Baptiste de Boyer, Marquis d'Éguilles"), unofficial French envoy to the Jacobites, had a low opinion of him and other senior Jacobites, describing Lochgarry as "a bandit", and suggesting George Murray was a British spy. For their part, the Scots were disillusioned by lack of meaningful English or French support, despite constant assurances of both.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcLynn1985177–181-122) Events also highlighted the reality that a low level, ongoing insurgency was far more cost-effective for the French than a restoration, a form of warfare potentially devastating to the local populace.[[d]](#cite_note-123) By exposing the divergence between Scottish, French and Stuart objectives, as well as the lack of support in England, the 1745 Rising ended Jacobitism as a viable political alternative in England and Scotland.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEColley198528-47)

The British authorities enacted a series of measures designed to prevent the Scottish Highlands being used for another rising. New forts were built, the [military road](/wiki/Old_military_roads_of_Scotland "Old military roads of Scotland") network finally completed and [William Roy](/wiki/William_Roy "William Roy") made the first comprehensive survey of the Highlands.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESeymour19804–9-124) Much of the power held by the Highland chiefs derived from their ability to require military service from their clansmen and even before 1745 the clan system had been under severe stress due to changing economic conditions; the [Heritable Jurisdictions Act](/wiki/Heritable_Jurisdictions_(Scotland)_Act_1746 "Heritable Jurisdictions (Scotland) Act 1746") removed such feudal controls by Highland chiefs.[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECampsie2017-125) This was far more significant than the better-known [Act of Proscription](/wiki/Act_of_Proscription "Act of Proscription") which outlawed Highland dress unless worn in military service: its impact is debated and the law was repealed in 1782.[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECampsie2017-125)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Charles_Edward_Stuart_%281775%29.jpg/250px-Charles_Edward_Stuart_%281775%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Charles_Edward_Stuart_(1775).jpg)

Charles Edward Stuart in old age; in 1759, he was dismissed by French ministers as "incapacitated by drink"

As early as 1745, the French were struggling with the costs of the [War of the Austrian Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Austrian_Succession "War of the Austrian Succession"), and in June 1746, they began peace negotiations with Britain at [Breda](/wiki/Congress_of_Breda "Congress of Breda"). Victories in [Flanders](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders") in 1747 and 1748 actually worsened their position by drawing in the previously neutral [Dutch Republic](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic"), whose shipping they relied on to avoid the British naval blockade.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlack199997–100-126) By 1748, food shortages among the French population made peace a matter of urgency, but the British refused to sign the [Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle](/wiki/Treaty_of_Aix-la-Chapelle_(1748) "Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle (1748)") while Charles remained in France. After he ignored requests to leave, the French lost patience; in December 1748, he was briefly jailed before being deported.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERiding2016496–497-127)

In June 1747, his brother [Henry](/wiki/Henry_Benedict_Stuart "Henry Benedict Stuart") became a Catholic priest; given that Charles had no legitimate heir, this was seen as tacit acceptance by their father James that the Jacobite cause was finished. Charles continued to explore options for a rising in England, including his conversion to Anglicanism, a proposal that had outraged his father James when previously suggested.[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECorp2011334-128) He "secretly" visited London in 1750 to meet supporters, and was inducted into the [Non Juror church](/wiki/Nonjuring_schism "Nonjuring schism").[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERobb2013-129) However, the decline of Jacobitism is demonstrated by the fact that King [George II](/wiki/George_II_of_Great_Britain "George II of Great Britain") and his government were well aware of Charles's presence and did nothing to intervene. The English Jacobites made it clear they would do nothing without foreign backing, which despite Charles's overtures to [Frederick II of Prussia](/wiki/Frederick_II_of_Prussia "Frederick II of Prussia") seemed unlikely.[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPittock1998123-130)

A plot to capture or assassinate George II, headed by [Alexander Murray of Elibank](/wiki/Alexander_Murray_of_Elibank "Alexander Murray of Elibank"), was betrayed to the government by [Alastair Ruadh MacDonnell](/wiki/Alastair_Ruadh_MacDonnell "Alastair Ruadh MacDonnell"), or "Pickle the Spy", but not before Charles had sent two exiles as agents. One was [Archibald Cameron](/wiki/Archibald_Cameron_of_Lochiel "Archibald Cameron of Lochiel"), responsible for recruiting the Cameron regiment in 1745, who was allegedly betrayed by his own clansmen and executed on 7 June 1753.[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELenman198027-131) In a 1754 dispute with the English conspirators, a drunken and increasingly desperate Charles threatened to publish their names for having "betrayed" him; most remaining English sympathisers now left the cause.[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMonod1993345-132)

During the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War") in 1759, Charles met [Choiseul](/wiki/%C3%89tienne_Fran%C3%A7ois,_duc_de_Choiseul "Étienne François, duc de Choiseul"), then [Chief minister of France](/wiki/Chief_minister_of_France "Chief minister of France") to discuss [another invasion](/wiki/Planned_French_invasion_of_Britain_(1759) "Planned French invasion of Britain (1759)"), but Choiseul dismissed him as "incapacitated by drink".[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZimmerman2003273-133) The Jacobite cause was abandoned by the French, while British supporters stopped providing funds; Charles, who had returned to Catholicism, now relied on the Papacy to fund his lifestyle. However, with the death of Charles's father in 1766, the Hanoverians received the Pope's *de facto* recognition.[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHamilton201557–58-134) Despite Henry's urgings, [Clement XIII](/wiki/Clement_XIII "Clement XIII") refused to recognise his brother as Charles III; Charles died of a stroke in Rome in January 1788, a disappointed and embittered man.[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPittock2004-135)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Monument_to_the_Stuarts_detail.JPG/250px-Monument_to_the_Stuarts_detail.JPG)](/wiki/File:Monument_to_the_Stuarts_detail.JPG)

Detail of the monument in the Vatican

Following Charles's death, Scottish Catholics swore allegiance to the House of Hanover, and resolved two years later to pray for King George by name. The Stuart claim passed to Henry, now a Cardinal, who styled himself King Henry IX of England. After he fell into financial difficulty during the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution"), he was granted a stipend by [George III](/wiki/George_III "George III"). However, his refusal to renounce his claim to be 'Henry IX' prevented a full reconciliation with the House of Hanover.[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohns1998161-136)

During the [Irish Rebellion of 1798](/wiki/Irish_Rebellion_of_1798 "Irish Rebellion of 1798"), headed by the [United Irishmen](/wiki/United_Irishmen "United Irishmen") with French support, the [Directory](/wiki/French_Directory "French Directory") suggested making Henry [King of the Irish](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Ireland "Monarchy of Ireland").[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPittock2006210-137)[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAston2002222-138) They hoped this would attract support from the Catholic Irish and lead to the creation of a stable pro-French client state. [Wolfe Tone](/wiki/Wolfe_Tone "Wolfe Tone"), the Protestant republican leader, rejected the suggestion, and a short-lived [Irish Republic](/wiki/Irish_Republic_(1798) "Irish Republic (1798)") was proclaimed instead.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAston2002222-138)

Following the death of Henry in 1807, the Jacobite claim passed to those excluded by the 1701 Act of Settlement. From 1807 to 1840, it was held by the [House of Savoy](/wiki/House_of_Savoy "House of Savoy"), then the [House of Habsburg-Lorraine](/wiki/House_of_Habsburg-Lorraine "House of Habsburg-Lorraine") until 1919, while the current Jacobite heir is [Franz, Duke of Bavaria](/wiki/Franz,_Duke_of_Bavaria "Franz, Duke of Bavaria"), from the [House of Wittelsbach](/wiki/House_of_Wittelsbach "House of Wittelsbach"). However, neither he nor any of his predecessors since 1807 have pursued their claim. Henry, Charles and James are memorialised in the [Monument to the Royal Stuarts](/wiki/Monument_to_the_Royal_Stuarts "Monument to the Royal Stuarts") in the Vatican.[[135]](#cite_note-139)

## Analysis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jacobitism&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Analysis")]

Traditional [Whig historiography](/wiki/Whig_historiography "Whig historiography") viewed Jacobitism as a marginal threat to the progression towards present-day Parliamentary democracy, taking the view that as it was defeated, it could never have won.[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESzechi19945-140) Representing "pre-industrial paternalism" and "mystical loyalism" against forward-thinking individualism, this conception of Jacobitism was reinforced by [Macaulay](/wiki/Thomas_Babington_Macaulay "Thomas Babington Macaulay")'s stereotype of the typical "Tory-Jacobite squire" as a "bigoted, ignorant, drunken philistine".[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESzechi19945-140)

More recent analyses, such as that of [J. C. D. Clark](/wiki/J._C._D._Clark "J. C. D. Clark"), suggest that Jacobitism can instead be regarded as part of a "deep vein of social and political conservatism running throughout British history", arguing that the Whig settlement was not as stable as has been depicted.[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESzechi19946-141) Further interest in Jacobite studies has been prompted by a reassessment of the nationalist aspirations of Scots Jacobites in particular, emphasising their place as part of an ongoing political debate.

### Romantic revival

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jacobitism&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Romantic revival")]

As the political danger of Jacobitism receded, the movement was increasingly viewed as a romantic symbol of the past, particularly the final rebellion. Relics and mementoes of 1745 were preserved, and Charles himself celebrated in "increasingly emotional language". This memorialising tendency was reinforced by the publication in the 1830s of selections from *The Lyon in Mourning* by [Robert Forbes](/wiki/Robert_Forbes_(bishop) "Robert Forbes (bishop)") (1708–1775), a collection of source material and interviews with Jacobite participants in the 1745 rising.[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPittock1998137-142)

19th-century historiography often presented Scottish Jacobites as primarily driven by a romantic attachment to the Stuarts, rather than the reality of individuals with disparate motives. This suited the Victorian depiction of Highlanders as a "martial race", distinguished by a tradition of a "misplaced loyalism" since transferred to the British crown.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPittock2009143-143) The participation of the Lowland Scots and north-eastern gentry was less emphasised, while his Irish Jacobite advisors like Captain [Félix O'Neille y O'Neille](/w/index.php?title=F%C3%A9lix_O%27Neille_y_O%27Neille&action=edit&redlink=1 "Félix O'Neille y O'Neille (page does not exist)") [[es](https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/F%C3%A9lix_O%27Neille_y_O%27Neille "es:Félix O'Neille y O'Neille")],[[140]](#cite_note-144) were, until very recently, inaccurately presented as worthless individuals who were solely a negative influence on Charles Stuart in 1745.[[141]](#cite_note-145)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Pettie_-_Jacobites%2C_1745.jpg/250px-Pettie_-_Jacobites%2C_1745.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pettie_-_Jacobites,_1745.jpg)

"Jacobites" by [John Pettie](/wiki/John_Pettie "John Pettie") (1874): romantic view of Jacobitism

Walter Scott, author of *[Waverley](/wiki/Waverley_(novel) "Waverley (novel)")*, a story of the 1745 rebellion, combined a romantic view of Jacobitism with a belief in what he says as the practical benefits of [Whiggism](/wiki/Whiggism "Whiggism"). In 1822 he arranged a pageantry of reinvented Scottish traditions for the [visit of King George IV to Scotland](/wiki/Visit_of_King_George_IV_to_Scotland "Visit of King George IV to Scotland"). The displays of [tartan](/wiki/Tartan "Tartan") proved immensely popular, and Highland clothing, previously associated with rebellion and regime change, became emblems of [Scottish national identity](/wiki/Scottish_national_identity "Scottish national identity"). Some descendants of those attained for rebellion had their titles restored in 1824, while persecutory and discriminatory laws against Catholics were [repealed](/wiki/Catholic_emancipation "Catholic emancipation") through the efforts of [Daniel O'Connell](/wiki/Daniel_O%27Connell "Daniel O'Connell") in 1829. With political and military Jacobitism now safely confined to an "earlier era", the hitherto largely ignored site of their final defeat at Culloden began to be celebrated.[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPittock2009146-146)

### Neo-Jacobite revival

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jacobitism&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Neo-Jacobite revival")]

Main article: [Neo-Jacobite Revival](/wiki/Neo-Jacobite_Revival "Neo-Jacobite Revival")

There was a brief revival of political Jacobitism in the late 1880s and into the 1890s.[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPittock2014?-147) A number of Jacobite clubs and societies were formed, starting with the [Order of the White Rose](/wiki/Order_of_the_White_Rose_(1886%E2%80%931915) "Order of the White Rose (1886–1915)") founded by [Bertram Ashburnham](/wiki/Bertram_Ashburnham,_5th_Earl_of_Ashburnham "Bertram Ashburnham, 5th Earl of Ashburnham") in 1886.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In 1890, [Herbert Vivian](/wiki/Herbert_Vivian "Herbert Vivian") and [Ruaraidh Erskine](/wiki/Ruaraidh_Erskine "Ruaraidh Erskine") co-founded a weekly newspaper, *The Whirlwind*, that espoused a Jacobite political view.[*[dead link](/wiki/Wikipedia:Link_rot "Wikipedia:Link rot")*] Vivian, Erskine and [Melville Henry Massue](/wiki/Melville_Henry_Massue "Melville Henry Massue") formed the [Legitimist Jacobite League of Great Britain and Ireland](/wiki/Legitimist_Jacobite_League_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "Legitimist Jacobite League of Great Britain and Ireland") in 1891, which lasted for several years. Vivian went on to stand for Parliament four times on a Jacobite platform – though he failed to be elected each time.[[144]](#cite_note-148) The revival largely came to an end with the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War"), and the various societies of the time are now represented by the [Royal Stuart Society](/wiki/Royal_Stuart_Society "Royal Stuart Society").

## Literary legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jacobitism&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Literary legacy")]

### English and Lowland Scots

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jacobitism&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: English and Lowland Scots")]

Many Jacobite folk songs emerged in Scotland in the [Romantic era](/wiki/Romantic_era "Romantic era"); a number of examples were collected by Scott's colleague [James Hogg](/wiki/James_Hogg "James Hogg") in his *Jacobite Reliques*, including several he likely composed himself. Scots poets such as [Sir Walter Scott](/wiki/Sir_Walter_Scott "Sir Walter Scott"), [Robert Burns](/wiki/Robert_Burns "Robert Burns"), [Alicia Ann Spottiswoode](/wiki/Alicia_Ann_Spottiswoode "Alicia Ann Spottiswoode"), [Agnes Maxwell MacLeod](/wiki/Agnes_Maxwell_MacLeod "Agnes Maxwell MacLeod"), and [Carolina Nairne, Lady Nairne](/wiki/Carolina_Nairne,_Lady_Nairne "Carolina Nairne, Lady Nairne") (whose "[Bonnie Charlie](/wiki/Bonnie_Charlie "Bonnie Charlie")" remains popular) added further examples. Many of these songs have seen a renewed popularity since the beginning of the [British folk revival](/wiki/British_folk_revival "British folk revival") in the 1950s.

Relatively few of the most famous surviving songs, except for those by [William Hamilton](/wiki/William_Hamilton_(Jacobite_poet) "William Hamilton (Jacobite poet)") and [James Robson](/wiki/James_Robson_(poet_and_songwriter) "James Robson (poet and songwriter)"), actually date from the time of the risings and the majority of those that do are in other languages than English or Scots.

### Irish language literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jacobitism&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Irish language literature")]

As demonstrated in 1924 by [Daniel Corkery](/wiki/Daniel_Corkery_(author) "Daniel Corkery (author)"), in [Modern literature in Irish](/wiki/Modern_literature_in_Irish "Modern literature in Irish"), Jacobitism inspired the enormously influential [Aisling](/wiki/Aisling "Aisling") or [dream vision](/wiki/Dream_vision "Dream vision") genre of [Irish bardic poetry](/wiki/Irish_bardic_poetry "Irish bardic poetry"). One of the most widely known is *[Mo Ghile Mear](/wiki/Mo_Ghile_Mear "Mo Ghile Mear")* by [Seán Clárach Mac Domhnaill](/wiki/Se%C3%A1n_Cl%C3%A1rach_Mac_Domhnaill "Seán Clárach Mac Domhnaill"). Other important Jacobite poets who composed immortal verse in [Munster Irish](/wiki/Munster_Irish "Munster Irish") during the 18th-century included [Aogán Ó Rathaille](/wiki/Aog%C3%A1n_%C3%93_Rathaille "Aogán Ó Rathaille"), [Éamonn an Chnoic](/wiki/%C3%89amonn_an_Chnoic "Éamonn an Chnoic"), [Tadhg Gaelach Ó Súilleabháin](/wiki/Tadhg_Gaelach_%C3%93_S%C3%BAilleabh%C3%A1in "Tadhg Gaelach Ó Súilleabháin"), [Eoghan Rua Ó Súilleabháin](/wiki/Eoghan_Rua_%C3%93_S%C3%BAilleabh%C3%A1in "Eoghan Rua Ó Súilleabháin"), and [Donnchadh Ruadh Mac Conmara](/wiki/Donnchadh_Ruadh_Mac_Conmara "Donnchadh Ruadh Mac Conmara"). The [Aisling](/wiki/Aisling "Aisling") poetic genre has also remained a living tradition and has been adapted, by poets such as [Máire Bhuidhe Ní Laoghaire](/wiki/M%C3%A1ire_Bhu%C3%AD_N%C3%AD_Laoghaire "Máire Bhuí Ní Laoghaire"), [Pádraig Phiarais Cúndún](/wiki/P%C3%A1draig_Phiarais_C%C3%BAnd%C3%BAn "Pádraig Phiarais Cúndún"), and [Seán Gaelach Ó Súilleabháin](/w/index.php?title=Se%C3%A1n_Gaelach_%C3%93_S%C3%BAilleabh%C3%A1in&action=edit&redlink=1 "Seán Gaelach Ó Súilleabháin (page does not exist)") to more recent causes and struggles by the [Irish people](/wiki/Irish_people "Irish people"). Since 1976, an Aisling poem by Liam mac Uistín in honour of "those who gave their lives for Irish freedom" has been permanently displayed at the [Garden of Remembrance](/wiki/Garden_of_Remembrance_(Dublin) "Garden of Remembrance (Dublin)") in [Dublin](/wiki/Dublin "Dublin").

### Scottish Gaelic literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jacobitism&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Scottish Gaelic literature")]

By 1933, [John Lorne Campbell](/wiki/John_Lorne_Campbell "John Lorne Campbell") published the groundbreaking book *Highland Songs of the Forty-Five*, consisting of 32 Gaelic song-poems, which were analyzed for political content, annotated, and published with facing translations into English [blank verse](/wiki/Blank_verse "Blank verse"), or unrhymed [iambic pentameter](/wiki/Iambic_pentameter "Iambic pentameter"); the real cultural, political, and religious reasons for the [Jacobite rising of 1745](/wiki/Jacobite_rising_of_1745 "Jacobite rising of 1745") had been obscured by the novels of Sir [Walter Scott](/wiki/Walter_Scott "Walter Scott") and [Robert Louis Stevenson](/wiki/Robert_Louis_Stevenson "Robert Louis Stevenson"), who both depicted, "the Highlander as a [romantic hero](/wiki/Tragic_hero "Tragic hero") fighting for a lost cause."[[145]](#cite_note-149) In response, Campbell set out to give, "a voice to the voiceless - ordinary men who had never been allowed to speak for themselves".[[146]](#cite_note-Ray_Perman_2013_Page_26-150)

As Campbell's volume and his later published writings revealed to the [English-speaking world](/wiki/Anglosphere "Anglosphere"), within [Scottish Gaelic literature](/wiki/Scottish_Gaelic_literature "Scottish Gaelic literature"), Jacobite ideology and the events of the risings inspired the immortal poetry of [Iain Lom](/wiki/Iain_Lom "Iain Lom"), [Sìleas na Ceapaich](/wiki/S%C3%ACleas_na_Ceapaich "Sìleas na Ceapaich"), [Alasdair Mac Mhaighstir Alasdair](/wiki/Alasdair_Mac_Mhaighstir_Alasdair "Alasdair Mac Mhaighstir Alasdair"), [Iain Mac Fhearchair](/wiki/Iain_Mac_Fhearchair "Iain Mac Fhearchair"), [Catriona Nic Fhearghais](/wiki/Catriona_Nic_Fhearghais "Catriona Nic Fhearghais"), [Iain Ruadh Stùibhart](/wiki/John_Roy_Stuart "John Roy Stuart"), and [William Ross](/wiki/William_Ross_(poet) "William Ross (poet)"). Despite also composing immortal poetry of his own about fighting for the government in the [Campbell of Argyll Militia](/wiki/Campbell_of_Argyll_Militia "Campbell of Argyll Militia") during the [Battle of Falkirk Muir](/wiki/Battle_of_Falkirk_Muir "Battle of Falkirk Muir") in 1746,[[147]](#cite_note-151) their contemporary, [Duncan Ban MacIntyre](/wiki/Duncan_Ban_MacIntyre "Duncan Ban MacIntyre"), offers in his later poetry, according to Campbell, "an interesting testimony to the bitter disillusionment of the Highlanders who had come to the aid of the Government, to be in the end treated no better that those who had rebelled against it."[[148]](#cite_note-152)

## Claimants to the thrones of England, Scotland, Ireland and France

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jacobitism&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Claimants to the thrones of England, Scotland, Ireland and France")]

Main article: [Jacobite succession](/wiki/Jacobite_succession "Jacobite succession")

* **[James II and VII](/wiki/James_II_of_England "James II of England")** (6 February 1685 – 16 September 1701).
* **[James III and VIII](/wiki/James_Francis_Edward_Stuart "James Francis Edward Stuart")** (16 September 1701 – 1 January 1766), James Francis Edward Stuart, also known as the *Chevalier de St. George*, the *King over the Water*, or the *Old Pretender*. (Son of James II)
* **[Charles III](/wiki/Charles_Edward_Stuart "Charles Edward Stuart")** (1 January 1766 – 31 January 1788), Charles Edward Stuart, also known as *Bonnie Prince Charlie*, the *Young Chevalier*, or the *Young Pretender*. (Son of James III)
* **[Henry IX and I](/wiki/Henry_Benedict_Stuart "Henry Benedict Stuart")** (31 January 1788 – 13 July 1807), Henry Benedict Stuart, also known as the *Cardinal King*. (Son of James III)

Since Henry's death, none of the Jacobite heirs have claimed the English or Scottish thrones. [Franz, Duke of Bavaria](/wiki/Franz,_Duke_of_Bavaria "Franz, Duke of Bavaria") (born 1933), a direct descendant of [Charles I](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England"), is the current legitimate heir of the house of Stuart. It has been suggested that a repeal of the [Act of Settlement 1701](/wiki/Act_of_Settlement_1701 "Act of Settlement 1701") could allow him to claim the throne, although he has expressed no interest in doing so.[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlleynede_Quetteville2008-153)

3. **[^](#cite_ref-3)** [/ˈdʒækəbaɪtɪzəm/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); [Scottish Gaelic](/wiki/Scottish_Gaelic "Scottish Gaelic"): *Seumasachas*, [[ˈʃeːməs̪əxəs̪]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Scottish_Gaelic "Help:IPA/Scottish Gaelic"); [Irish](/wiki/Irish_language "Irish language"): *Seacaibíteachas*, *Séamusachas*; the term derives from *[Jacobus](/wiki/Jacobus_(name) "Jacobus (name)")*, the [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") version of the first name of [James II of England](/wiki/James_II_of_England "James II of England"), which is rendered in [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") as *[Jacobus](/wiki/Jacobus_(name) "Jacobus (name)")*, and its followers, known as **Jacobites**.
4. **[^](#cite_ref-123)** Summarised in a British intelligence report of 1755: "...'tis not in the interest of France the House of Stuart shoud ever be restored, as it would only unite the three Kingdoms against Them; England would have no exterior [threat] to mind, and [...] prevent any of its Descendants (the Stuarts) attempting anything against the Libertys or Religion of the People."
